speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four. Irene, you were telling me about this girl you met in Lviv. Her name was Helen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, Helen, when I met her, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was telling me about he father that was killed by the Germans. That he was not in any organization but they were partisans. And if partisans did something to even the car of a German, then the German did go to their first homes and pull out men and shot them. And so she say she's alone with her mother and they are having very hard time. They don't have money and this. So I did have enough food from the office when I served so I shared it with them. And soon Helen told me that she was engaged to a Jewish man, Henry Weinbaum. And that they were supposed to get married and now the Germans took him away. She doesn't know where. And she just tried to find if they're bringing new Jewish people from the neighborhoods. You know, she tries to find it out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one day she said to me, Irene, there is a new group coming. And I know somebody said that you know that Henry is dead. So she asked me to go with her. So I, and Jeanina, my sister, and her mother, we took a bus. I told the Shultz, that I finded friends and that I would like to spend it with her some time. And we went to that-- not far from Lviv, from the center of the town. You know, we walked maybe three, four, five blocks. And when we come there, there were many people there already. There were barbed wire fencing and barbed wire gate. And of a second the SS step out and start shooting in the air. And [GERMAN]! You go out! And chasing everybody. Chasing everybody. I was so scared. We were scared so we start running. And there was an empty house. And we got-- from the back was open and the window were broken and we were on the first floor. And instead run out completely, we were in that house. So we witnessed a nightmare. We witnessed that SS were really pushing, and beating, and kicking a mass of people. And it was unbelievable. And we did even see the SS. A man, probably the wife was Jewish and he was not, he was running. It was a beautiful blonde woman with a little tiny girl, maybe three-years old. And she was holding her by the hand. The child was yelling, screaming. And the man was trying to get to it, Well, the SS shot him and people with walking-- you know, trying to walk around him, but laying. There was another woman that I'll never forget. She did a little-- she was wounded in her leg and the little girl was screaming, crying. She was trying to protect her. There was a old man with a white beard, white head-- it looks like a rabbi to me. You know, unbelievable pictures. And we were just standing there and watching. And then I did see a SS man pull a baby from mother's arm. It was a little infant. The baby was crying. And he just took the baby and threw it head to the ground. I did see another man. He put the baby in the air and shot like a bird. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was unbelievable. Jeanina-- my sister was crying. I was standing there bewildered. We were scared to go out or anything. Finally, we did see the procession leaving. And as we were coming down, we could hear shooting from far away. We follow. We could not go too far. And they pushed them through the town. Behind the town was a dug, shallow grave. They use machine gun. The lucky ones, they were dead. Because the earth was quivering with the breath of those that were buried alive. I never forget. Without speaking, without talking we parted. Helen went one way and I with my sister. We did have to go to Schultz. When we come back, we look, what, like-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] crying. Red eyes and with headache. We were looking like we're sick. So I told her that we did have a bad news from home from my parents and we are upset. So he even was so kind that give us off a little, you know, to-- And now we were moving to Tarnopol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to Tarnopol, tell me a little bit about Schultz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Schultz was a short, stocky guy, with the red cheeks. A kind man. He was kind, really. I didn't see him abusing or hurting anyone.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a solider. He was not Gestapo. He was a Wehrmacht. And he showed me his wife and children picture. [INAUDIBLE] always say, we're fighting. Children and women instead fighting, you know, the Russians. We have to fight solider to solider. So he was doing the job because he was taken, but he did not take the pleasure in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was the head of the kitchen. He did have the responsibility to-- over the cooks. Because we did not cook in the place where I was serving. There was brought-- was a special kitchen when people worked on this. They brought in the food. We were just distributing, and dressing up, and putting on a table, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were working, that was a hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we were in Lviv. That was a smaller place.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but before you got to Lviv.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a big hotel in Radom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who lived in that hotel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was empty. The German took it. I mean, the German, when they decided they just threw everybody and took it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was like a German headquarters?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct. For the Major, Major Rugamer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you got to Tarnopol.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now when we're coming to Tarnopol. It's another story. It was like a place-- a fence of about three city blocks. And end of that it was like a factory where they were doing the-- preparing the ammunition and cars for the front. And there was working about 100 maybe Jewish people in that. In a front, from one street, because from front of the street there was that big hotel with a very big, maybe three-foot thick walls-- old, you know, them. And there was a dining room. And there was a also recreation-like room for the Germans. And there was a little room that was given to me by the diner. I received that little room there. And I share with my sister. In this big hotel there were about 12 or 14 officers. Each of them did have like a suite, one room with the bathtub and this. Naturally, the bathtubs-- we did have heated water. You know, you did this to-- there were a long thing. And the Major did live on the third floor. Was about five or six floors but no elevator. This was old but, anyway. And I was on a downstairs where is the diner. Next to the diner I did have my room. The officers all have the keys to go through the front door. But, like, people were brought in. There were on three sides they did have posted soldiers that they did not let everybody-- was have to register who goes in. And then in the center was there another building that was there, a washing room for the officers and secretaries. They took to them wash their shirts but also to fix the dresses, fix their uniforms, you know, to dressmaker and tailor's, to iron, to clean it. Well, I was given the room by the diner. And the Major then, about maybe week or so later, he told me-- Schultz told me-- that Major wanted give me another responsibility, to take care of the laundry room for his officers and secretaries. He was pleased with me. And I served dinners, you know, and breakfast and lunch.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I went to the where they're washing the clothes, I met 12 Jewish people. I never knew them before. At first, you know, they were scared of me. You know, who I am and this.

speaker: interviewee
text: But soon as I was getting the laundry to them, and I told them I am alone, separated from my family, you know. And I-- they start trusting me. They trusted me. They start telling me story of their broken lives. There was Ida and Leizer Heller, the oldest couple. Then there was a Franka Zylberman. Was my age, her faced like an angel. She was beautiful. And we two-- maybe because we are the two youngest-- we just have a spark for each other. And she was very courageous girl. She did have three sisters and her parents in the ghetto. And there was this Steiner. And there was the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Paola and Heshu Marx. They were really the-- Schneider Liwszyc. He was a student, a doctor student really. And, you know, there was 12 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I started going, they start trusting me. We knew that I didn't come to do any harm or any-- blonde, blue-eyed with the name of Gut, you know? So then the Major at night a special-- because breakfast, lunch, they were not exciting. But at night they did have a dinner and a drink, and they entertaining. The girls was sitting and this.

speaker: interviewee
text: I noticed that head of the Gestapo, [INAUDIBLE] Rokita, was coming for dinner. He was very handsome, but devil. I mean, mean. And I start listening. I spoke quite good German by now. I start listening to the conversation. I did not planning it. It just evolved, you know. And I wanted help so much. So there I heard Rokita said, Herr Major, on Thursday don't count on the Jews to come to work. The Major was-- he say, look, the people, my people, are needed because I am very important for the front. And he said, well, I have my order. I have to thin them out. So that's what he say. They have to-- Well, at first I did not realize that. But then I did see, you know, some of the people didn't come. Not to the laundry room, but to the-- where they were preparing the ammunition and that. Well, I start-- if I listen, I start carrying the information to the laundry room. Soon, without knowing what we were doing, we created grapevine information center. I become the ears and the eyes for the persecuted people working there. In the meantime, my sister in the hotel, they-- one time Schultz say, we needed-- the officers having-- they wanted some of the girls to clean. My sister could not do it. There was about 12 apartments, you know. She could not do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember when I mention to Schultz, he said, tell the Major. And Major said, Irene, if you want to pick up some Jewish girls to come. I say, Herr Major, who I am, you know, to select this? And but I told in the laundry room. Well, soon everybody has somebody they met and least and I give it to Schultz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about six girls came. And my sister, Jeanina, was telling them work as long-- you know, slow-- but work long so the time passes and you don't need to go early to work or so. And food she was bringing from the kitchen. You know, we did have a plenty bread, and butter, and cake, and fruit. And everything they have in lapels, you know, and covered with the towels. You know, they were cleaning. And I was doing the same with my friends in the laundry room. It was going fine. Well, also, Henry Weinbaum now. He was working in the laundry room first, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that that's the man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not really-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I first when I met Helen-- Helen came to Tarnopol. She knew that I am in Tarnopol and she came. And we mentioned the name. And I made-- Schultz allowed me to have a friend. He said that I have a friend, Helen, that she will come to my little room there to visit. So we knew it. And it was very scary, but very satisfying, you know. Helen now took a job on the farm. One of the Major's officer through-- I don't know if they were Jewish people, or perhaps other Christian people, or whoever. Polish people. They threw them out. And she took a job on the farm as a managing farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we did have horses, and buggy. We did have slates, you know? We did have potatoes. We did have. And we were now trying to help many people just before action. They wanted leave. So Helen or I-- we find where they were hiding on their way in bushes, so in park, and we're helping people to escape. What is an action?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was an action?

speaker: interviewee
text: Action is action that the Gestapo say this day they will go and whoever they capture they will kill. Or that they said we send them to another work. Usually they have it excuse. That's the action, you know. And so we were able to warm people before action. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one moment. Did they have a chance? If they knew that there's going to be an action, did they have a chance to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if they knew day or two ahead, many, some of them, run away or hide. Some of them we were able to help to get to the forest. But people-- you see, Francis-- I mean Franka, Franka Silberman. I was cleaning the Major's office. And on his desk I noticed stack of permits with a stamp from the Gestapo for the Jewish people or that to go out, or go to ghetto because they could not go, those people that worked, into the ghettos, too. Well, I did have the idea when I was cleaning some of these I just put in my pocket. So and I knew now that there will be action.

speaker: interviewer
text: Franka did go to ghetto. She was courageous, you see? She did have the courage to go. And she went to the ghetto and warmed the people there that this and this day there will be action. Don't go out, or hide, or do something, you know, because there will be.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also those from laundry room, the other, were telling in the barracks, you know. And we were able to save some lives, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was like a network.

speaker: interviewee
text: Grapevine information center. I was the ears and the eyes. And I could not do it myself, you know, like to go here and there. There are people that work in the laundry room that they work. Schultz now-- the people were complaining it was cold and the German took all coats and furs and everything from the Jewish people, you know. And so I ask Schultz for blankets. And they made it little caps from, you know, the-- because in the laundry room-- in the laundry room now we did have an idea because more and more I find out that there will be raids. For them when there's raids, to go-- to them that is death. We were creating in the laundry room a little hiding place. The whole wall was covered with the shelves. And so some of the shelves, you know, we made it very shallow, just maybe three, four. Very shallow. Folded the blankets so that this. And behind that there was a small place for hiding. So a few times, some did not go back to the-- and they hid during-- for the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, Francis went to ghetto and she did not come back. And I find out that there were raids. I was besides myself. What will I do? So I asked Major that one of the dressmaker took the dresses that the secretaries, German, will come to try. And she took it to ghetto to use a different machine. So I ask him if I may go. So, naturally, I have the cart and I went. And there was a Gestapo standing right in this. Was just after action. There is no way I can tell you. Naturally, I say I am Irena Gut. I put an-- I realize now that I am a pretty young girl, speak German, with blonde and blue eyed, so I start playing the role.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he let me in. I went there. Walking in that ghetto right after action. Sha. Nobody. Just here and there you can see a curtain move or there's something in the window. People scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: I go to that home where her parents live. Well, they told me in the laundry room-- Ida and the other, they knew. I opened the door. Was a dark, musty. I say, Franka? Franka? Nobody answer. I said, Franka, Irene. All of a sudden somebody grabbed-- I was by the step. Somebody grabbed me by the legs. And that was Franka. She was just-- the eyes big. She was wild. Said, what you doing here? There is a raid. It's a raid. My parents were taken.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, she was unbelievable. She could not say two words without yelling, screaming. I said, Franka, it's after. I am here. I came to take you. I don't-- She don't want to go. She said, my parents and my sisters-- I don't want to go. So I say, you must. Your parents get killed there. You have to survive. Somebody has to tell what happened. I say, go, get some dresses. Put something in a basket. Finally, I took her back. Again, when I was-- I say I have a permit for the dressmaker, you know. And they let me go through. I brought her to the laundry room. Now everybody-- and she was just like dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you brought her out--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the ghetto and brought her to the laundry room back. So everybody was-- you know, there is so many times. They told what is happening in the work barracks, how the SS men, you know, after they come from work for hours. And he was really telling that to the Major.

speaker: interviewer
text: I overheard, too, that he is teaching them to work. They put him for two, three hours stand, you know, not doing nothing, you know? And if somebody move or something, got shot or they're beat to death. And the Major I remember sometimes saying, why should you do that? I need my workers. They have to prepare the work for the front because now the Russians were fighting the Germans already, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an unbelievable stress. And also, from time to time, Helen put the potatoes, whatever I could steal, and I did it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I was serving dinner to the German officers and secretaries, I pleaded with them that I have a big family. I was like a Volksdeutsche, you know? I have family and they are hungry. And I have nephews, and nieces. And please if you don't use your rationing tickets, would you please give it to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was pretty. I spoke. So they gave it to me. With Schultz I did go to the baron houses to buy food for the kitchen, you know, for--

speaker: interviewer
text: So I also told Schultz. I say I have a family, you know? And so I have the rationing tickets. So I bought them blankets. I bought them warm stocking and shoes because in forest they were cold. And I was able to get some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Helen did have potatoes and other things. We were delivering, dropping off. The bread was growing in a forest. We were dropping off that.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will wind up this tape. And when we come back, I would like you could go back for a moment and tell me how was it with being with your sister under these conditions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I will tell you.
